run.output:
Q1 Results:
  Rows output: 4
  Rows processed: 59142609
  Execution time: 55 ms (average of 5 runs: 54, 56, 55, 56, 58 ms)

comparison_result.error: 3 rows have floating-point precision differences (ULP differences, not functional)

profiling.note: perf not available in environment; cache analysis from code inspection

Q1.notes: Query completes in 55ms (excellent performance), processes 59.1M rows. Results are functionally correct but have floating-point precision formatting differences. 64 cores utilized with morsel-driven parallel aggregation.

Q1.bottleneck: Floating-point precision in computed aggregates (sum_disc_price, sum_charge); implementation produces slightly different precision due to order-dependent floating-point arithmetic
Q1.root_cause: Parallel aggregation with thread-local hash tables introduces non-deterministic summation order. When merging partial results from 64 threads, the accumulation order of floating-point values differs from the expected sequential order, causing ULP (Unit in Last Place) differences in the final result.

analysis.overall: Q1 is highly optimized: already implements parallel morsel-driven aggregation with 64 threads, thread-local hash tables to avoid synchronization, mmap-based columnar I/O with MADV_SEQUENTIAL, and efficient memory layout. Execution time (55ms) is 3.5x faster than baseline PostgreSQL (15,460ms avg) and comparable to DuckDB (84-157ms range, avg 108ms). Primary optimization opportunity is not performance but correctness: floating-point precision formatting needs to match ground truth exactly for validation.

critical_fixes.issue: Floating-point precision differences in computed aggregates (sum_disc_price, sum_charge)
critical_fixes.fix: Increase floating-point output precision formatting from 4-6 decimal places to full double precision (15+ significant digits), and/or compute aggregates in deterministic sequential order or use compensated summation (Kahan summation) to match expected precision
critical_fixes.description: The code outputs with limited precision (lines 270-277 in q1.cpp use setprecision(4) for sum_disc_price and setprecision(6) for sum_charge), but the ground truth expects different precision levels. Additionally, thread-local parallel accumulation produces order-dependent floating-point errors. Solutions: (1) Use std::setprecision(15) for doubles to match IEEE 754 representation, (2) implement Kahan summation algorithm in AggState to minimize floating-point error accumulation, or (3) collect all matching rows in a single sequential pass instead of parallel reduction to match ground truth order.

performance_optimizations.description: Replace scalar date comparisons (line 182: `if (shipdates[i] > CUTOFF_DATE) continue;`) with SIMD batch comparisons using AVX-512. Current code processes 1 row per iteration with a branch instruction. With AVX-512, process 16 int32 values per instruction (512-bit / 32-bit = 16 elements), producing a bitmask of matching rows. This reduces instruction count and branch mispredictions. Implementation: use _mm512_cmpgt_epi32 to compare 16 dates in parallel, then extract selection vector for selective loading of subsequent columns.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~1.2-1.5x speedup on filtering phase (assuming 98% selectivity means 98% of rows pass). Reduces branch mispredictions from ~1 per row to ~1 per 16 rows. Frees ALU for aggregate computation.

performance_optimizations.technique: Compact Hash Table (Swiss Table or Robin Hood)
performance_optimizations.description: Replace std::unordered_map with a Swiss Table or Robin Hood hash table implementation (Abseil FlatHashMap or folly::F14FastMap). Current code uses std::unordered_map for thread-local aggregation (line 160), which has poor cache locality due to chaining (linked lists). Swiss Tables store 16 control bytes per group and use SIMD lookups for O(1) amortized time with better cache behavior. Robin Hood hashing minimizes probe distances for more predictable latency.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~1.1-1.3x speedup on hash table operations (lookup/insertion). Current code performs ~59M lookups; compact tables reduce memory access latency and cache misses.

performance_optimizations.technique: Loop Unrolling and Compiler Optimization Hints
performance_optimizations.description: Add pragmas to inner loop (lines 180-206) to hint to the compiler for better optimization: `#pragma omp simd` or `#pragma GCC ivdep` to enable auto-vectorization. Manually unroll the morsel loop to process 4-8 rows per iteration, reducing loop overhead and improving instruction-level parallelism. For example, batch 4 consecutive rows, compute their derived values in parallel (disc_price, charge), then insert into hash table. This amortizes loop control overhead.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~1.05-1.2x speedup from reduced loop overhead and better compiler optimization. Depends on compiler ability to vectorize.

performance_optimizations.description: The code uses mmap with MADV_SEQUENTIAL (line 100), which is good. Further optimization: manually prefetch 64 bytes ahead in the hot loop (lines 180-206) to hide memory latency. Use __builtin_prefetch(shipdates + i + 16) to prefetch the next cache line before it's accessed. Also consider cache-aligning the AggState structure (currently 64 bytes but unaligned) to prevent false sharing between threads when local_agg vectors are laid out.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~1.05-1.1x speedup from hidden memory latency. Mainly beneficial if cache misses are significant.

performance_optimizations.description: Q1 input (lineitem) is already sorted by l_shipdate (per storage_design.json). Instead of hash aggregation, implement a streaming sorted aggregation that processes rows in order and emits groups when the (returnflag, linestatus) key changes. This would require: (1) reading rows in l_shipdate order, (2) detecting group boundaries, (3) emitting results incrementally. Pros: eliminates hash table overhead, O(n) time, O(1) memory. Cons: requires pre-sorted I/O, adds complexity. Only viable if input ordering is guaranteed.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~1.2-1.5x speedup if sorted aggregation can be implemented without additional sort. Eliminates hash table allocation and lookup overhead. Reduces memory footprint significantly.

summary: Q1 is already highly optimized: parallel morsel-driven execution with 64 threads, thread-local hash tables, mmap columnar I/O, and achieves 55ms execution on 59M rows (3.5x faster than PostgreSQL). The primary issue is floating-point precision formatting that doesn't match ground truth expectations—a semantic fix, not a performance issue. Performance optimizations beyond the current baseline are incremental (1.05-1.5x range) and involve SIMD vectorization of date filtering, compact hash tables, and loop optimizations. The query is already near-optimal for a hash aggregation approach. Recommended next steps: (1) fix floating-point precision to pass validation (critical), (2) implement SIMD date filtering for 1.2-1.5x improvement (priority 1), (3) consider sorted aggregation if data ordering can be leveraged (priority 5, medium risk).
